speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're getting oriented to something you'll use constantly in the workshop: your Learning Room. Think of it as your personal practice repository on GitHub, built so you can learn the full workflow safely and realistically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just a folder somewhere. It's an actual GitHub repository with issues, branches, pull requests, and automation?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. When you accept the GitHub Classroom assignment, GitHub makes a private copy of the workshop template repository for you. That copy lives in the workshop organization and is named something like learning-room-your-username. You get write access to that repo. You can create branches there, open pull requests there, and receive automated feedback there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And just to clear up a very common beginner question: am I supposed to work in the original template repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: No. You do not work directly in the shared template. The template is the clean source maintained by facilitators. Your work happens only in your own private Learning Room repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So each student gets a separate copy that starts with the same files, but our work stays separate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You won't see other students' work inside your repo, and they won't see yours by default. That separation is intentional. It gives you a safe place to practice. It also means you do not need to create your own GitHub organization or change repository permissions. The facilitators handle the organization, the Classroom setup, the template, and the automation permissions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why go to the trouble of giving every student a separate repo instead of putting everyone in one shared practice repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three big reasons: safety, authenticity, and pace. Safety means you can experiment, make mistakes, and recover without affecting anyone else. Authenticity means you still practice real GitHub work: issues, branches, pull requests, reviews, checks, and merges. And pace means you can move through the Day 1 challenges at the speed that works for you. You're not blocking other students, and they aren't blocking you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's private and low risk, but still close to real open source work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the idea. Later you'll contribute in a more public, shared setting. The Learning Room is where you build muscle memory first: issue, branch, pull request, review, merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk through getting into it, because I know this first setup moment can feel stressful.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. The starting point is the Day 1 GitHub Classroom assignment link. Before you begin, make sure you're signed into your GitHub account in your browser. You'll also need the assignment link from the facilitator. It usually looks like classroom dot github dot com slash a slash some random ID.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What happens when I open that link?

speaker: ALEX
text: You'll land on a page hosted on classroom dot github dot com. Your screen reader should announce a heading with the assignment name, something like the workshop title and Day 1. If GitHub Classroom asks for authorization to access your GitHub account, activate the Authorize GitHub Classroom button. That's usually a one-time step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And sometimes it asks you to identify yourself from a roster, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If that page appears, use arrow keys through the list, or use Find in Page with Control F or Command F to search for your name. Activate the control next to your name. If you can't find your name, choose the link to skip to the next step, then tell the facilitator in chat so they can reconcile the roster afterward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comes the actual accept button.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Activate Accept this assignment, or Accept the assignment. After that, you'll get a status page. It may say something like, You are ready to go. Behind the scenes, GitHub Classroom is copying the learning-room-template repository into the workshop organization and granting you access. That usually takes about ten to thirty seconds.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is one of those places where a screen reader user may need a little extra patience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. The page may not automatically announce when your repository is ready. Refresh the page every fifteen seconds or so, or use browse mode and press K to move through links until you hear a link to your new repository. The repository URL will look like github dot com slash workshop-org slash learning-room-your-username.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once I open that repository, what should I confirm?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three things. First, the repository name matches learning-room-your-username. Second, the About area or repository description confirms it's your private workshop copy. Third, you can see folders and files that came from the template, such as docs, dot github, and README dot md. And bookmark this page, because you'll return to it repeatedly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the first challenge?

speaker: ALEX
text: When your Learning Room is ready, Challenge 1 should appear as an issue in your repository. Facilitators prepare that through the progression bot. From the repository page, go to the Issues tab. If you know GitHub keyboard shortcuts, G then I opens Issues. Look for an open issue with a title like Challenge 1: Find Your Way Around, often authored by a bot account like aria-bot or github-actions bot.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the issue body tells you what to do next.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It explains the task, where to find evidence, and how to submit your completion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if Challenge 1 doesn't show up?

speaker: ALEX
text: If it's still missing after about two minutes, refresh the Issues tab once. If it's still not there, open the Actions tab and check whether the Student Progression Bot workflow ran successfully. If it failed or never ran, send the facilitator the link to your repository in workshop chat. They can trigger the student-progression workflow or create Challenge 1 from the issue template.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And before we leave setup, we also want to confirm Gandalf exists.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Gandalf is the pull request validation bot. Open the Actions tab and look for a workflow named pr-validation-bot or Gandalf PR Validation. You don't need to run it yet. You just want to confirm the workflow is present so you know automated PR feedback is wired up.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So by the end of this first setup sequence, I should have my own private repo, at least one challenge issue, and visible automation workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great summary. And this part of the curriculum is really orientation. There are no challenges in this chapter itself, and no automation check tied directly to it. It's here to prepare you for the issue-based challenges that begin next.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there a readiness check before moving on?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Before starting the challenge chapters, you should be able to do three things: find docs slash CHALLENGES dot md in your Learning Room repository, explain the flow issue to branch to pull request to review to merge, and identify where Gandalf's bot feedback appears on a pull request, which is the Conversation tab.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about the two-track idea, because this can be confusing. There are the GitHub Skills modules, and then there's the Learning Room.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. These are two separate but reinforcing tracks, both using your own GitHub account. Track one is the GitHub Skills modules. Those are optional and self-paced. Examples include Introduction to GitHub, Communicate Using Markdown, and Review Pull Requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Those are the ones where GitHub's learning bot walks you through steps?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In that track, the automated guide is Mona. The purpose there is focused skill practice: creating a branch, opening a pull request, merging, writing markdown, reviewing pull requests, and similar basics.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the second track is the required workshop track in the Learning Room.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's where the workshop's Day 1 challenge sequence lives. Early blocks focus on things like finding your way around the repository, filing issues, branching, committing, opening a pull request, and even handling a merge conflict. Later blocks move into collaboration culture, merging, and community tools like labels, milestones, and notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So one track is optional skill drills, and the other is the required guided practice environment for the workshop.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a clean way to say it. They complement each other, but they are not the same. One important learning card idea here is: two tracks, one account. You may use the same GitHub account for both, but the context, bots, and goals differ.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we compare them directly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Skills modules are public or personal learning exercises managed by GitHub's own teaching flow, usually in repositories created for those modules. The Learning Room is your private workshop repository in the classroom organization. Skills modules are optional. The Learning Room is required for the workshop. Mona guides the Skills modules; Gandalf and the progression system support the Learning Room. And the Learning Room is sequenced around workshop challenges, peer practice, and accessibility-focused open source contribution habits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's get concrete about what's inside the repo. What's the folder structure for?

speaker: ALEX
text: At a high level, your Learning Room contains documentation files you'll edit, automation files under dot github, a README, and a challenge menu. The docs folder is especially important because that's where much of your practice work lives.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And before editing, we need a practice branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This matters a lot. You create a separate branch so your work is isolated from the main branch. That protects the stable version of the repository and lets you package your changes in a pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the branch is like a safe workspace where I can try things before proposing them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's the reason for a separate branch: isolation, reviewability, and alignment with standard GitHub flow. You don't make challenge edits directly on main unless a specific exercise tells you to. Instead, create your branch, switch to it, make your changes there, and then open a pull request back into main.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should learners think about using that branch day to day?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually like this: read the issue, create a branch named for the task, switch to that branch, make and save your changes, commit them, push if you're working locally, and open a pull request. If you're editing directly on GitHub's website, GitHub often helps you create a branch as part of that edit flow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also a practical tool-card idea here: know how to switch to your practice branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. In GitHub's web interface, branch switching often happens through the branch selector near the top of the file list. In VS Code or the command line, you switch branches with the tools available there. The essential concept is not memorizing every interface immediately. It's knowing what branch you're on before you start editing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's tour the practice files, because these are the materials students actually work on.

speaker: ALEX
text: One important file is docs slash welcome dot md. This introduces open source contribution. It includes specific TODO sections learners complete.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are those TODOs about?

speaker: ALEX
text: TODO 1 is the section called Who Can Contribute? The key idea is that open source contribution is not limited to expert programmers. People contribute through code, documentation, testing, design, accessibility feedback, issue triage, reproduction steps, community support, and more.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's especially important in accessibility work, where lived experience and careful observation are genuine expertise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. TODO 2 is about Finding Something to Work On. Learners should understand that contributors often start by reading project documentation, looking for labeled issues, exploring beginner-friendly tasks, checking contribution guides, and noticing gaps in clarity or accessibility. In other words, useful work is often discoverable if you know where to look.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And TODO 3?

speaker: ALEX
text: After Your Contribution Is Merged. That section helps learners see merging not as the end of participation, but as one step in an ongoing relationship with a project. After a contribution is merged, you might verify the change, thank reviewers, continue following the repository, help with future issues, or make another contribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So even that file is doing double duty: teaching content and giving practice with editing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Another practice file is docs slash keyboard-shortcuts dot md, which serves as a screen reader shortcut reference. That's especially relevant for learners who need efficient keyboard-based navigation in GitHub, browser content, and related tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also docs slash setup-guide dot md.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That file is about getting ready to contribute. It reinforces preparation habits: making sure your tools are ready, understanding the repository workflow, and knowing what you need before participating in a contribution process.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then docs slash CHALLENGES dot md is basically the menu of what's available.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. It's the challenge menu for your Learning Room. Even though challenges may unlock progressively through issues, that file helps you understand the landscape of the exercises.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are bonus challenges too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Bonus challenges extend practice beyond the required path. They're useful if you finish early, want extra repetition, or want to stretch into more realistic contribution scenarios.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about pull request sharing, because this is where learners often wonder, Wait, if my repo is private and personal, how do review and collaboration happen?

speaker: ALEX
text: Great question. The Learning Room is private by default, but collaboration is still practiced through a structured flow. Here's the general sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step one: the student opens a pull request.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Imagine Student A is working on Challenge 3, completing the welcome guide. They create or update a branch, make the required edits, and open a pull request from that branch into main in their own repository. That pull request becomes the place where changes, comments, and checks come together.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then automation validates it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. A workflow such as dot github slash workflows slash learning-room-pr-bot dot yml can run validation. Gandalf or a related automation reviews whether the pull request appears to meet the exercise expectations and posts educational feedback.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Not just pass or fail, but teaching feedback.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In a learning environment, the bot is there to coach, not just gatekeep.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comes peer review, but facilitator-arranged.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Because repositories are private, peer review access is intentionally arranged. Visibility is controlled. Facilitators may pair students and grant the needed access so one learner can review another learner's pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not a free-for-all where everyone sees everything.

speaker: ALEX
text: No. Access is managed. Next, the reviewer reads and comments. If you're the assigned reviewer, you open the paired student's pull request, read the changed files, and leave comments or an overall review. Again, visibility is limited to those who were intentionally given access.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then the author responds and updates.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you're the pull request author, you read the feedback, decide what revisions to make, update your branch, and let the pull request reflect those new commits. That response process is part of the learning. And once more, visibility is controlled within the arrangement set by facilitators.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then we get to merge and celebration.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. When the review is approved, or when the workflow for that exercise allows self-merge, the author merges the pull request. After merge, a progression bot can post the next challenge issue. That's how the learning path advances one step at a time. The visibility of all of this remains scoped to the people who need access.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So one learning card idea here is that pull request sharing is structured, intentional, and supported by both bots and people.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the visibility piece really explicit. What do you see, and what do your peers see?

speaker: ALEX
text: By default, you see your own repository contents: your issues, your branches, your pull requests, your checks, and your bot feedback. Other students do not automatically see your work. Peer visibility happens only when facilitators intentionally pair or grant access for a review activity. That's a key privacy and learning design feature.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone is nervous about making mistakes in front of the whole class, this setup reduces that pressure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You still practice authentic review, but without unnecessary exposure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we break down the learning automation system itself?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. There are three main kinds of feedback timing in this environment. Type 1 is automated bot feedback, often within about thirty seconds. That's the fast response you may see after opening or updating a pull request. It checks structure, expected edits, or workflow conditions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Type 2 is peer reviewer feedback.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. That usually takes longer, maybe fifteen to sixty minutes depending on workshop timing and pairing. This is human feedback: interpretation, encouragement, suggestions, and questions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Type 3?

speaker: ALEX
text: Progress tracking on merge. When a pull request is merged, the system can update your progression by posting the next challenge or marking your advancement. That creates a clear sense that merging isn't just administrative. It actually moves your workshop journey forward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about study groups?

speaker: ALEX
text: Study groups are optional. Learners may discuss concepts, compare notes, or work alongside each other while still making their own repository changes. For example, two students might read the same challenge together over a call, talk through what a pull request is, and then each complete the task separately in their own Learning Room.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So collaboration in understanding is welcome, even when repository work remains individual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's revisit the key differences between a Skills module and the Learning Room, because that's likely to appear on learners' mental quiz later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the essentials. Skills modules are optional, self-paced, and guided by GitHub's bot, Mona. The Learning Room is required for the workshop, lives in the workshop organization, and is private to you unless access is intentionally shared. Skills modules focus on isolated skills. The Learning Room ties those skills into a coherent contribution workflow with issues, branching, pull requests, review, merge, and progression through workshop challenges.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm wondering, Which one counts for the workshop? The answer is the Learning Room.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's help learners review a peer's pull request well, because many people are more nervous about reviewing than authoring.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's common. First, finding pull requests to review usually happens through facilitator pairing or explicit assignment, not random browsing. If you are assigned a review, you'll receive the repository and pull request link you need.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once I'm in the assigned pull request, how should I read it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start with the pull request title and description so you know the goal. Then inspect the changed files. Compare what's in the pull request against the challenge instructions. Ask: Did the author address the requested sections? Is the writing clear? Did they preserve formatting? Are there accessibility or clarity improvements to suggest?

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when leaving a review?

speaker: ALEX
text: Be specific, respectful, and actionable. Point to exact lines when possible. Distinguish between required changes and optional suggestions. If something works well, say that too. Good review is not just error detection; it's communication.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about responding to feedback as the author?

speaker: ALEX
text: Read comments carefully. If the suggestion makes sense, update your branch and reply briefly so the reviewer knows what changed. If you're confused, ask a clarifying question. If you disagree, respond respectfully and explain your reasoning. The learning card here is that pull request sharing is a conversation, not a verdict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the FAQ lightning round.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's do it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can I see other students' pull requests?

speaker: ALEX
text: Not by default. Repositories are private, and access is intentionally limited unless facilitators pair you for review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if I don't agree with my assigned reviewer?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start by asking clarifying questions and explaining your reasoning respectfully. If needed, involve a facilitator. Disagreement can be part of healthy review, especially when handled thoughtfully.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Will my pull request get lost when everyone is working at once?

speaker: ALEX
text: In this setup, no. Your work is in your own repository, and progression is tracked there. The whole design reduces the chaos of one giant shared queue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can I comment on someone else's pull request?

speaker: ALEX
text: Only if you've been given access, such as through an assigned review pairing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if my reviewer doesn't respond?

speaker: ALEX
text: Wait a reasonable amount of time, then contact the facilitator. Remember that peer feedback may take fifteen to sixty minutes depending on workshop flow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can I work with a friend?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, in the sense of discussing ideas or studying together, especially in optional study groups. But each learner should complete their own repository work unless the exercise explicitly says otherwise.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How long does review take?

speaker: ALEX
text: Bot feedback is usually fast, around thirty seconds. Human review varies more, often fifteen to sixty minutes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if bot feedback is wrong?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat the bot as helpful automation, not infallible authority. Recheck the instructions, inspect your pull request, and ask a facilitator if the bot seems mistaken.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Do I need to complete every challenge?

speaker: ALEX
text: Follow the workshop requirements for required challenges first. Bonus challenges are extra. If you're unsure what is expected, check the challenge menu or ask the facilitators.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If a learner gets stuck anywhere in this process, what's the best advice?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pause and locate where the process broke. Is it account access, Classroom acceptance, finding the repo, missing issues, branch confusion, or pull request feedback? Then use the available support: reread the issue, check the Actions tab, inspect the Conversation tab on the pull request, consult the challenge menu, ask your study group if you're using one, and message the facilitator in chat when needed. Getting stuck is normal in GitHub workflows. The important skill is learning how to diagnose the stuck point.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's reassuring, especially for learners who may be balancing a screen reader, browser navigation, new terminology, and GitHub's interface all at once.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Friction is not failure. It's part of learning a real tool.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I also want to pause on the emotional message of this chapter. The repository is called the Learning Room for a reason.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This is a place to practice contribution, not a test you must perform perfectly in public. By opening issues, creating branches, editing files, opening pull requests, receiving feedback, and merging, you are already doing the work of contributing. That matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So even before the larger open source project work, this counts as real contribution practice.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. You're learning the habits and structures that make open source collaboration possible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can you mention the trusted references behind this material?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The chapter points learners toward authoritative GitHub documentation, including topics like README files, editing files, GitHub flow, pull requests, and general Git and GitHub docs. Those references are useful when you want the official platform explanation alongside the workshop guidance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the workshop gives the learning path, and the official docs provide deeper confirmation and detail.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Put together, they make a strong support system.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. So your next move is simple: make sure your Learning Room exists, confirm Challenge 1 is there, know where docs slash CHALLENGES dot md lives, and be ready to move through the flow from issue to branch to pull request to review to merge.

speaker: ALEX
text: And remember: your Learning Room is private, personal, and purposeful. It's your place to build confidence before contributing in bigger shared spaces.
